food_subgroup: Seafood noodle dish — glass noodles with vannamei prawns in Thai-style sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — used as closest parent for prawn-based mixed dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Thai prawn glass noodle dish. A0F8T (crustaceans and products thereof) used as closest defensible anchor given the primary protein component is vannamei prawn.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli dish.

preparation_method: braised in clay pot or wok

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of glass noodles topped with whole vannamei prawns in a savoury Thai-style sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp (mixed species) cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Glass noodles (mung bean starch) cooked — USDA FoodData Central; composite dish estimation weighted by ingredient proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked vannamei/mixed shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked mung bean glass noodles (FDC ID 169726), weighted at approximately 45% prawns, 40% glass noodles, 15% sauce/aromatics by cooked weight. Sauce contribution (oyster sauce, fish sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger) estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a restaurant-prepared Thai prawn glass noodle dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mung bean glass noodles / cellophane noodles GI reference; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli as a composite dish. GI estimated at 39 based on the primary carbohydrate source: mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles), which have a reported GI of approximately 33–45 (mean ~39) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The prawn and sauce components contribute negligible glycaemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (39 × 46.2 g) / 100 ≈ 18.0, rounded to 18.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 1,680 mg sodium — over 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from mung bean glass noodles despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of mung bean glass noodles (GI ~39) is favourable for blood glucose management, but the glycemic load per serving (~18) is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vannamei prawns contribute meaningful dietary cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g) and the dish is high in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request lower-sodium sauce preparation to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Vannamei Prawn Vermicelli (วุ้นเส้นกุ้งแวนนาไม) is a Thai seafood noodle dish featuring whole vannamei prawns (Litopenaeus vannamei) served over or braised with mung bean glass noodles in a savoury, aromatic Thai-style sauce. The dish is typically prepared by briefly marinating the prawns in garlic, ginger, oyster sauce, fish sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, then steaming or stir-frying them together with pre-soaked glass noodles until the noodles absorb the sauce and become translucent and silky. The result is a dish with tender, sweet, slightly briny prawns contrasting with soft, slippery, flavour-saturated glass noodles. Aromatics such as spring onion, coriander, and fresh chilli are typically added as garnish. Vannamei prawns are the world's most commercially farmed shrimp species and are prized for their mild sweetness, firm texture, and high protein content. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, with a strong protein contribution (~9.8 g/100 g) from the prawns, moderate carbohydrate (~13.2 g/100 g) primarily from the glass noodles, and low fat (~2.4 g/100 g). The mung bean glass noodles have a low glycemic index (~39), making the dish relatively favourable for blood glucose management compared to rice or wheat noodle alternatives. However, the dish is high in sodium due to the fish sauce and oyster sauce used in the seasoning, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional form, though oyster sauce may contain trace gluten. It is commonly served as a main course in Thai restaurants and seafood establishments in Singapore and Thailand.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Thai vannamei prawn vermicelli exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked vannamei shrimp and mung bean glass noodles, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is estimated from the analogous mung bean glass noodle GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked vannamei/mixed shrimp and cooked mung bean glass noodles, weighted at approximately 45% prawns, 40% glass noodles, and 15% sauce/aromatics. Restaurant preparation will vary. 2. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on the amount of fish sauce and oyster sauce used; the estimate of 480 mg/100 g reflects a moderately seasoned preparation. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the primary components (shrimp and mung bean noodles). 6. GI estimated at 39 based on mung bean glass noodles as the primary glycaemic carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 7. The dish is sold at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 37.80. 8. Halal status is uncertain as oyster sauce and fish sauce sourcing is not confirmed; marked is_halal: false by default. 9. Gluten-free status is uncertain due to potential gluten in oyster sauce; marked is_gluten_free: false by default.
